title: Influence of Coherent Elastic Strain on Phase Separation in BCC Nb-V Alloys
abstract: Coherent elastic strain is an important but often neglected contribution to phase-separation thermodynamics in alloy systems where decomposed phases have appreciable lattice mismatch. We develop a thermodynamic framework that incorporates coherent elastic compatibility directly into phase-diagram calculations alongside conventional CALPHAD chemical free energies. Applied to the BCC Nb-V system, the framework shows that coherent elasticity substantially suppresses phase separation, narrows the miscibility gap, and lowers the critical temperature toward experimentally observed values. Beyond these quantitative effects, the coherent constraint qualitatively alters the interpretation of phase equilibria: the equilibrium decomposition compositions become functions of both temperature and overall alloy composition, so the two-phase boundary no longer represents unique coexistence compositions. These results establish coherent elasticity as a key thermodynamic factor in lattice-mismatched systems and provide a general framework for coherent phase-diagram modeling.
summary: This paper develops a thermodynamic model to show that coherent elastic strain is a critical factor in suppressing phase separation in lattice-mismatched alloys. By incorporating elastic compatibility into phase-diagram calculations, the authors successfully align theoretical predictions for the Nb-V system with experimental observations. The work demonstrates that elastic constraints fundamentally change the nature of phase equilibria by making coexistence compositions dependent on the total alloy composition.
main_problem: Investigating how coherent elastic strain influences phase separation thermodynamics and the width of the miscibility gap in alloys with significant lattice mismatch.
main_result: Coherent elasticity substantially suppresses phase separation, narrows the miscibility gap, and lowers the critical temperature, while making equilibrium compositions dependent on the overall alloy composition.
method: A thermodynamic framework combining DFT-derived chemical free energies with explicit coherent elastic contributions using both isotropic and biaxial strain models.
model_or_system: The BCC Niobium-Vanadium (Nb-V) binary alloy system, modeled using a combination of CALPHAD-style chemical free energies and mechanical compatibility constraints (isotropic and biaxial).
key_observables: Coherent binodal and spinodal boundaries, critical temperature (Tc), miscibility gap width, and equilibrium phase compositions (x1, x2).
important_parameters: Lattice mismatch, temperature (T), overall alloy composition (X), and elastic parameters (bulk modulus, pressure derivative, and biaxial expansion coefficients).
main_assumptions: Neglect of PV terms and electronic entropy; approximation of vibrational entropy as linear in composition; assumption of an ideal random-solution configurational entropy.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows composition dependence of properties; Figure 2 shows the energy surface as a function of biaxial strain; Figure 4 compares decomposition compositions in chemical-only vs. coherent-elastic models; Figure 5 analyzes the elastic origin of the suppression mechanism.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of lattice mismatch in phase separation, presents a thermodynamic framework integrating chemical and elastic energies, compares isotropic and biaxial models, validates the model against DFT and experimental data, and discusses the qualitative shift in phase boundary interpretation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Composition dependence of the fitted isotropic Birch–Murnaghan equation-of-state parameters for BCC Nb–V alloys. Blue circles denote the discrete parameter values ob- tained from independent Birch–Murnaghan fits to the DFT energy–volume data at sampled compositions, while solid lines represent the second-order Redlich–Kister interpolations. Pan- els show (a) equilibrium energy per atom E0, (b) equilibrium atomic volume V0, (c) bulk modulus B0, and (d) pressure derivative of the bulk modulus B′ 0. The smooth trends and excellent agreement between discrete data and continuous fits confirm the robustness of the composition-dependent isotropic energetic model.
Figure 2: Energy as a function of biaxial strain for Nb0.444V0.556. Symbols denote DFT data, and the surface corresponds to the fitted third-order model.

Figure 3: Comparison of phase stability predicted by the chemical-only model and by the coherent-elastic model. Yellow: both models predict phase separation; orange: only the chemical-only model predicts two-phase equilibrium; blue: single-phase region. Dashed curves denote spinodal boundaries. Coherent elastic effects significantly reduce both the miscibility gap and the spinodal.

Figure 4: Equilibrium decomposition compositions under chemical-only and coherent-elastic descriptions. (a) Chemical-only model: all overall compositions X within the miscibility gap decompose into the same pair (x1, x2) at a given temperature, giving horizontal tie-lines consistent with the lever rule. (b) Coherent-elastic model: the decomposition compositions become functions of X. Increasing XV at fixed temperature shifts x1 upward and x2 down- ward, indicating weaker decomposition on the V-rich side due to the larger coherent elastic penalty. (c) Temperature dependence of decomposition trajectories for multiple XV. Under coherent constraint, each overall composition follows a distinct...

Figure 5: Analysis of the elastic origin of the composition-dependent phase behavior under coherent constraint. (a) Free-energy decomposition at T = 800 K, showing the chemical driving force ∆Gchem, the elastic contribution of each phase, and the resulting total ∆Gtotal. (b) Phase-resolved elastic energies per atom as a function of overall composition for different temperatures. The variation with temperature is indicated by the color intensity. (c) Phase- resolved in-plane Green–Lagrange strain f∥as a function of overall composition for different temperatures. The variation with temperature is indicated by the color intensity.
